content.text: is a technique in which an API server — most commonly an LLM inference endpoint — sends its output to the client

as it is generated, rather than buffering the entire result in memory and dispatching it in one reply. Instead of the client opening a connection, waiting in silence for seconds or minutes, and then receiving a complete blob of text, the server opens a persistent HTTP connection and pushes small chunks of data — typically individual tokens or short token sequences — as each one becomes available.

In the context of agentic AI, streaming responses matter at every layer of the stack. When an autonomous agent calls an LLM to reason about a problem, the model does not produce its answer instantaneously. Autoregressive language models sample one token at a time, each conditioned on all previous tokens. The generation of a 1,000-token response at 50 tokens per second takes 20 seconds. Without streaming the user stares at a blank screen for 20 seconds; with streaming the first token appears in roughly 200–400 ms and reading begins immediately. For agent orchestration frameworks, streaming also unlocks the ability to inspect partial outputs — detect tool calls mid-generation, cancel runaway reasoning chains early, or route different completion branches in parallel.
Technically, the most common delivery mechanism is

over HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2. Each event is a newline-delimited text frame carrying a JSON payload. The Anthropic Messages API, the OpenAI Chat Completions API, and virtually every major LLM provider surface streaming via this mechanism. WebSockets are a less common but valid alternative when bi-directional communication is also required.
The core problem streaming solves is
perceived and actual latency in long-running LLM calls

. The two latency metrics that define the user experience of an LLM-powered product are Time to First Token (TTFT) and Time to Last Token (TTLT, or total generation time). Non-streaming responses collapse these two into a single event — TTLT — making users wait the full generation time before seeing anything. Streaming separates them: TTFT becomes the delay before the first character appears on screen, and TTLT remains the same but is now invisible to the user because they are already reading.

Beyond raw perception, streaming enables architectural patterns that simply do not exist in batch mode. An agent orchestrator can begin parsing a partially-generated tool-call JSON before it is complete, fire off the tool request the moment the parameters are available, and overlap network round-trips. A safety filter can scan the token stream in real time and interrupt generation the instant a policy violation is detected, saving compute cost and eliminating the need to discard a fully-generated unsafe reply. A UI can animate a typing indicator synchronized to the actual generation rate, making the product feel alive rather than frozen.

From a business perspective, streaming also improves infrastructure efficiency. When responses are long, a streaming architecture lets the server pipeline multiple generation requests through the GPU without holding completed results in memory waiting for the downstream client to become ready. The connection stays open and drains continuously, reducing peak memory pressure on both the inference server and the proxy layer.

Users start reading after TTFT (commonly 150–500 ms), not after TTLT (often 5–30 s for long responses). Human perception treats text that arrives progressively as fundamentally faster than text that appears all at once.

Chat products, coding assistants, and voice interfaces can render tokens as they arrive, creating a conversational feel. The user can interrupt mid-generation if the model heads in the wrong direction.

If the first few sentences of a response are clearly wrong or off-topic, an agent (or the user) can cancel the stream. The inference provider bills only for the tokens generated up to cancellation, not the full output budget.

An orchestrator can begin executing a tool call as soon as the tool-call JSON is complete, while the model is still generating its reasoning text. This overlaps LLM generation time with tool execution time, reducing wall-clock latency for multi-step agentic workflows.
The server does not need to buffer the full response before sending. At scale, eliminating response buffering meaningfully reduces RAM footprint on both the inference server and reverse proxies.

Middleware can log or meter the stream at the token level, enabling fine-grained cost accounting (tokens in vs. tokens out) and latency histograms (TTFT, median inter-token latency, tail latency of last token).

Long-lived HTTP connections require careful timeout configuration on every hop: load balancers, API gateways, CDNs, and reverse proxies all have default idle timeouts (commonly 60–300 s) that will close a streaming connection prematurely unless explicitly configured to match the maximum expected generation time.
If the client reads chunks slowly — common in mobile apps on weak connections — the server-side write buffer fills. You need explicit flow-control logic or risk dropped chunks or connection resets.

In a batch API, a non-200 HTTP status is the complete signal of failure. In a stream, an error can occur mid-stream after a 200 status line has already been sent. You must inspect error events inside the stream, not just the HTTP status code.

Each SSE event typically carries one token or a small chunk. If you need the full text for post-processing (logging, content moderation, structured parsing), you must accumulate chunks into a buffer and process it at stream end. This is extra code and introduces a memory high-water mark.
Idempotency and retry semantics are broken.

If a stream fails at token 300 of a 500-token response, you cannot resume from token 300 — you must start the generation from scratch. This is inherent to autoregressive generation; partial outputs are not safely resumable.
Not universally supported by serverless runtimes.
Some runtimes (AWS Lambda with default API Gateway integration) buffer the entire response before delivery. Streaming requires explicit opt-in configuration or alternative deployment targets.

Think of streaming responses like a live radio broadcast versus a podcast download. A podcast downloads the full episode before you can press play — all or nothing. A radio broadcast starts the moment the station transmits; you hear each word as it is spoken. The broadcaster cannot go back and fix a mistake mid-sentence; you cannot skip ahead. But you can change the channel (cancel the stream) the moment you hear something you do not want.

Extend the analogy to LLMs: the language model is the broadcaster, the inference server is the transmitter tower, and your application is the radio receiver. The broadcaster generates speech one syllable at a time (one token at a time). Without streaming, someone at the tower records the broadcast, waits for the show to end, then hands you the recording. With streaming, the tower retransmits each syllable the moment it is spoken. The total amount of speech is identical — streaming does not make the model faster — but your experience is completely different.
A second mental model useful for agentic systems: think of the token stream as a

. An agent supervisor reading this channel can make decisions in real time — fire a tool call the moment parameters are complete, trigger an alarm if the model begins hallucinating a known false fact, or update a progress indicator. In batch mode that supervisor is blind until the broadcast is completely over.
At the HTTP level, a streaming LLM response uses
(HTTP/1.1) or HTTP/2 DATA frames to send response body bytes incrementally without knowing the Content-Length up front. The client keeps the TCP connection open and processes bytes as they arrive.
, an HTML5 standard (also usable from plain HTTP clients) with a simple text-based framing protocol. Each event looks like:
line carries a JSON payload. Two consecutive blank lines delimit events. The special event type
Understanding why streaming is natural for LLMs requires understanding autoregressive generation. A transformer language model produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary at each step
The model samples (or argmax-selects) one token
from that distribution, appends it to the context, and repeats. This is inherently
completes. There is no batch of output tokens to accumulate; each token is ready one at a time. Streaming is therefore not a post-processing trick but a direct window into the generation loop itself.
The inter-token latency — the time between consecutive tokens — follows an approximately constant rate once the model reaches its cruising speed. If we define inter-token latency as
, the expected total generation time is:
Typical values for hosted LLM APIs: TTFT is 150–600 ms, inter-token latency
is 15–30 ms (roughly 40–70 tokens/second). For a 500-token response this gives a total generation time of roughly 8–15 seconds — long enough that non-streaming is genuinely painful.

The diagram below illustrates the full request-response lifecycle for a streaming LLM call, from the client application through an optional server proxy to the LLM API, and the chunk-by-chunk return path.

attrs.alt: Streaming Responses Architecture: Client sends HTTP POST with stream:true, optional proxy forwards to LLM API, SSE chunks flow back token by token. Bottom panel shows token-by-token timeline comparing non-streaming (blocked until all done) vs streaming (tokens appear immediately).

content.text: The client sends a single HTTP POST
in the request body. The server responds with status 200 and a
header before any tokens are generated.

receives the upstream SSE stream from the LLM API and forwards each chunk downstream to the browser client. This is the standard architecture for web applications that need to protect their LLM API keys — the browser never calls the LLM API directly.

— the first token arrives quickly. The non-streaming row in the timeline shows the client blocked for the entire generation duration. The streaming row shows tokens arriving progressively, with TTFT marking the first token.
in both modes. Streaming does not accelerate the model — it only moves the user-visible wait from TTLT to TTFT.

— chat, writing assistant, code editor, conversational agent — where the user waits for the model's reply. Even a modest improvement in perceived latency dramatically increases perceived product quality.
detect and act on tool calls
before the full response is generated. Streaming is mandatory for this pattern.
and you want to give users the ability to interrupt or redirect generation.
— checking the output as it arrives against a policy ruleset, and killing the connection if a violation is detected.
metering costs at the token level
and need fine-grained per-request token accounting.
— generating embeddings, processing thousands of documents offline, running evaluation pipelines. The complexity of streaming adds zero benefit when no human is watching.
Your infrastructure does not support long-lived connections. Some
(AWS Lambda via API Gateway without response streaming enabled) buffer the entire response before delivery.
that must be fully validated before processing — for example, when parsing structured JSON from the model. In this case you must buffer the stream anyway, so batch mode is simpler.
downstream consumer is a queue or database
— not a human or real-time process. Writing a stream to Kafka or Postgres is equivalent to batch at the consumer level.
Step-by-step with the Anthropic Python SDK
Create a client and open a streaming context manager using
Iterate over text events inside the context. The SDK handles SSE parsing, reconnection logic, and final message assembly.
after the loop exits, or use
Verify usage metrics on the final message:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

# Stream a response token by token
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain gradient descent in 3 paragraphs."}],
) as stream:
    for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
        # text_chunk is a string — may be one word, one character, or a few tokens
        print(text_chunk, end="", flush=True)  # flush=True so each chunk appears immediately

# After the context exits, the final assembled message is available
final_message = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"\nInput tokens:  {final_message.usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens: {final_message.usage.output_tokens}")
print(f"Stop reason:   {final_message.stop_reason}")

This shows a complete production-ready FastAPI endpoint that receives a user message, calls the Anthropic API with streaming, and forwards the SSE stream to the browser client. This pattern protects your API key server-side.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import anthropic
import json

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment


def generate_stream(user_message: str):
    """Generator that yields SSE-formatted chunks to the browser."""
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    ) as stream:
        for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
            # Format as SSE — browsers parse this natively via EventSource
            payload = json.dumps({"text": text_chunk})
            yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"

        # Send a [DONE] sentinel so the client knows to close the connection
        yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"


@app.post("/chat/stream")
async def chat_stream(body: dict):
    user_message = body.get("message", "")
    return StreamingResponse(
        generate_stream(user_message),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disables nginx response buffering
        },
    )

# curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/chat/stream \
#      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
#      -d '{"message": "What is a transformer?"}' \
#      --no-buffer
When you need access to the raw SSE events — useful for logging, structured output parsing, or tool call detection — iterate over the stream directly rather than using

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "List 5 Python standard library modules with one-line descriptions."}],
) as stream:
    for event in stream:
        event_type = type(event).__name__
        if event_type == "RawContentBlockDeltaEvent":
            delta = event.delta
            if hasattr(delta, "text"):
                print(delta.text, end="", flush=True)
        elif event_type == "RawMessageStopEvent":
            print("\n[Stream complete]")

msg = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"Tokens used: {msg.usage.input_tokens} in, {msg.usage.output_tokens} out")
Example 2 — Async Streaming with Tool Call Detection
This example demonstrates how streaming enables detecting a tool call
the full response is generated. An agent can launch a tool call the moment its JSON is complete, overlapping LLM generation time with tool execution time.

import anthropic
import asyncio

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()


def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    # In a real implementation, this would call a weather API
    return f"The weather in {city} is 22 C and sunny."


async def stream_with_tool_detection():
    tools = [
        {
            "name": "get_weather",
            "description": "Get the current weather for a city.",
            "input_schema": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["city"],
            },
        }
    ]

    async with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        tools=tools,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the weather in Tokyo right now?"}],
    ) as stream:
        async for chunk in stream.text_stream:
            print(chunk, end="", flush=True)

    # After stream ends, inspect the final message for tool calls
    final = stream.get_final_message()
    if final.stop_reason == "tool_use":
        for block in final.content:
            if block.type == "tool_use":
                print(f"\n[Tool called: {block.name}({block.input})]")
                result = get_weather(block.input["city"])
                print(f"[Tool result: {result}]")


asyncio.run(stream_with_tool_detection())
Real-World Example — Production Chat with Metrics

The following pattern is representative of how streaming is deployed in production chat products serving thousands of concurrent users. The critical design decisions are: never buffer the full response on the server, propagate backpressure from the downstream client to the upstream LLM connection, and emit metrics at the chunk level for SLO tracking.

import anthropic
import time
import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import AsyncGenerator

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()


@dataclass
class StreamMetrics:
    request_start: float = field(default_factory=time.monotonic)
    first_token_at: float = 0.0
    last_token_at: float = 0.0
    token_count: int = 0
    cancelled: bool = False

    @property
    def ttft_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.first_token_at - self.request_start) * 1000

    @property
    def total_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.last_token_at - self.request_start) * 1000

    @property
    def tokens_per_second(self) -> float:
        duration = self.last_token_at - self.first_token_at
        return self.token_count / duration if duration > 0 else 0


async def production_stream(
    user_id: str,
    conversation: list[dict],
    max_tokens: int = 2048,
) -> AsyncGenerator[str, None]:
    metrics = StreamMetrics()

    try:
        async with client.messages.stream(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            system="You are a helpful assistant.",
            messages=conversation,
        ) as stream:
            async for chunk in stream.text_stream:
                now = time.monotonic()
                if metrics.token_count == 0:
                    metrics.first_token_at = now
                metrics.last_token_at = now
                metrics.token_count += 1
                yield chunk   # FastAPI / Starlette flushes this immediately

    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        metrics.cancelled = True
        raise
    finally:
        # Emit to your observability backend (e.g., Datadog, Prometheus)
        print(
            f"user={user_id} "
            f"ttft={metrics.ttft_ms:.0f}ms "
            f"total={metrics.total_ms:.0f}ms "
            f"tps={metrics.tokens_per_second:.1f} "
            f"tokens={metrics.token_count} "
            f"cancelled={metrics.cancelled}"
        )

# In a real FastAPI app:
# @app.post("/v1/chat")
# async def chat(req: ChatRequest, request: Request):
#     async def event_gen():
#         async for chunk in production_stream(req.user_id, req.messages):
#             if await request.is_disconnected():
#                 break  # stop generating when client disconnects
#             yield f"data: {json.dumps({'text': chunk})}\n\n"
#         yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"
#     return StreamingResponse(event_gen(), media_type="text/event-stream")

At scale (10,000+ concurrent users), the key infrastructure constraints are: LLM API concurrency limits (shared across all requests in your organization), TCP socket file descriptors (each streaming connection holds a file descriptor for its entire duration), and reverse proxy timeout configuration (nginx's
must exceed your maximum expected generation time, typically 120–300 s).
Mistake 1 — Buffering the Entire Stream Before Sending

A common anti-pattern is collecting all chunks into a list and then yielding them at the end, which completely defeats the purpose of streaming. The user still waits for the full generation to complete before seeing anything.

Because the HTTP status 200 is sent before generation begins, an error during generation will not produce a non-200 HTTP response. You must check for error events inside the stream and catch exceptions from the SDK.

# WRONG: checking only HTTP status — misses mid-stream errors
import httpx
response = httpx.post(url, json=payload)
if response.status_code == 200:  # always True even if stream errored mid-way
    process(response.text)

# CORRECT: use the SDK, which raises typed exceptions for stream errors
with client.messages.stream(...) as stream:
    try:
        for chunk in stream.text_stream:
            yield chunk
    except anthropic.APIStatusError as e:
        # Handles both initial HTTP errors and mid-stream API errors
        log_error(e)
        raise
    except anthropic.APIConnectionError as e:
        # Network-level disconnection mid-stream
        log_disconnection(e)
        raise
Without explicit configuration, nginx (and most load balancers) will close the connection after 60 seconds of no new data. For long LLM responses, this silently truncates the output mid-stream.
Always use the SDK, not raw HTTP, for production code.

The Anthropic and OpenAI SDKs handle SSE parsing edge cases, reconnection semantics, error event detection, and final message assembly. Rolling your own SSE parser introduces subtle bugs around partial UTF-8 sequences and event boundaries.
Instrument TTFT and inter-token latency separately.

These metrics reflect fundamentally different parts of the system: TTFT captures queuing and model startup; inter-token latency captures GPU throughput. Alert on both independently with separate SLO thresholds.
When a browser tab closes mid-stream, detect the disconnect (
in FastAPI) and cancel the upstream API call. This avoids paying for tokens no user will ever see.
Even with streaming, set an absolute wall-clock timeout on your upstream call (e.g., 120 s) so a misbehaving call cannot hold a connection open indefinitely.
Do not mix streaming and structured output parsing in the same path.
If you need to parse JSON from the model response, accumulate the stream into a buffer, validate at end-of-stream, then process. Do not attempt to parse JSON on a live token stream.
on all streaming responses. These headers are required by the SSE specification and prevent proxies and CDNs from caching or buffering the event stream.
Use browser DevTools network throttling or
on Linux to test your streaming UI under poor network conditions. Backpressure bugs and rendering glitches only surface under these conditions.
For a streaming chat product, the practical user-visible latency budget has four components:

attrs.latex: \text{TTFT}_{\text{user}} = t_{\text{network (up)}} + t_{\text{queue}} + t_{\text{prefill}} + t_{\text{network (first token down)}}

content.text: : time for the request to reach the API. Typically 20–100 ms depending on geography.
: time waiting for a GPU slot at the inference provider. Highly variable; can spike to seconds under heavy load or tight rate limits.
: time for the transformer to process the entire input prompt in parallel. Grows roughly linearly with input token count. A 10,000-token system prompt may add 500–2,000 ms.
: TCP round-trip for the first SSE frame. Typically 20–80 ms.
Streaming does not change these numbers — it just makes them visible earlier, so the user starts reading rather than staring at a spinner.

Each active streaming connection holds a TCP socket and an in-flight API call for its full duration. At 50 tokens/second and an average 400-token response, a connection lasts about 8 seconds. To sustain 1,000 concurrent users you need 1,000 simultaneous open API connections plus corresponding LLM API concurrency quota. Horizontal scaling of your proxy server is straightforward (stateless), but LLM API concurrency limits are the hard ceiling.
internally. For high-concurrency async applications, configure the pool explicitly:

import anthropic
import httpx

http_client = httpx.AsyncClient(
    limits=httpx.Limits(
        max_connections=500,
        max_keepalive_connections=100,
        keepalive_expiry=30,
    ),
    timeout=httpx.Timeout(
        connect=5.0,
        read=120.0,    # must exceed maximum expected generation time
        write=10.0,
        pool=5.0,
    ),
)

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(http_client=http_client)
context manager for sync and async. Handles all SSE parsing and exposes
on all LLM wrappers; callbacks via
or custom handlers. Useful for chaining streaming output through multi-step agents.
with an async generator handles chunked transfer encoding automatically.

provides a unified streaming interface across 100+ LLM providers. Useful for building provider-agnostic agentic frameworks.
— Invaluable for debugging SSE streams at the network level. Inspect the raw

The main alternative data delivery patterns for LLM responses are: standard batch HTTP, WebSockets, long polling, and gRPC streaming. The table below compares them across the dimensions that matter most for LLM-powered applications.

For the overwhelming majority of LLM use cases, SSE over HTTP is the correct choice: it is universally supported, requires no protocol upgrade, is trivially proxied by all standard infrastructure, and perfectly matches the one-directional (server-push) nature of LLM token generation. WebSockets are the right upgrade only when you also need client-to-server messages on the same persistent connection, such as voice interfaces where user speech arrives simultaneously with model speech.

The most common mistake is calling the LLM API directly from browser JavaScript, exposing your API key in source code. Always proxy through your server. The server holds the key as an environment variable; the browser authenticates to your server using session tokens.

If your streaming UI renders HTML or Markdown from the LLM response without sanitization, a malicious LLM output could inject executable code into the UI. Sanitize all rendered content, especially links and HTML tags, regardless of source.

Streaming endpoints that do not authenticate users can be abused by bots to drain your LLM API quota. Add per-user or per-IP rate limiting at your proxy layer, and require authentication before opening any stream.

If your server logs the raw SSE stream for debugging, partial responses may contain sensitive information the model was in the middle of generating. Treat the token stream with the same sensitivity as the complete response.

The TCP connection can drop at any point — network interruption, server restart, or client navigation. Implement a retry protocol at the application level: on reconnect, send the full conversation history and regenerate from scratch. There is no partial-resume capability in current LLM APIs.
or similar mid-stream. The SDK surfaces these as typed exceptions. Implement exponential backoff and retry, noting that you must re-generate from token 0.

If your downstream client reads chunks slowly, the kernel TCP send buffer fills. Monitor for socket errors on the write path and implement async write semantics — this is handled automatically by asyncio and frameworks like Starlette.
Always implement a non-streaming fallback. If your proxy detects that SSE is not supported by the client (missing
header), fall back to a batch request and return the full response as JSON. The user experience degrades gracefully to pre-streaming behavior.
Users begin reading after the first token (hundreds of milliseconds), not after the last token (many seconds). This is the single most impactful UX improvement available for LLM-powered products.
Total generation time is identical with or without streaming.
Streaming does not make the model faster — it removes the artificial wait by pipelining output delivery alongside generation.
SSE over HTTP is the right default choice.

It requires no protocol upgrade, is universally supported by infrastructure, and precisely matches the server-push pattern of LLM token generation. Use WebSockets only when you also need client-to-server messages on the same connection.
Always proxy the LLM API through your server.
Never expose your API key to browser clients. Your server authenticates inbound users and proxies the outgoing LLM stream, forwarding chunks to the browser as they arrive.
Configure every hop for long-lived connections.
Load balancers, nginx, CDNs, and API gateways all have default idle timeouts that will silently kill long streams. Set
explicitly on every proxy in the chain.

Detecting a tool call mid-stream and launching it before generation completes is one of the most powerful techniques in agentic AI — it overlaps LLM time with tool execution time, reducing the wall-clock latency of multi-step workflows.
A 200 HTTP status does not mean the stream will complete successfully. Error events arrive inside the SSE stream. Use the SDK, which raises typed exceptions for these cases.
Instrument TTFT and tokens-per-second separately in production.
These metrics reveal different system bottlenecks: TTFT reflects queuing and prefill; tokens-per-second reflects GPU throughput. Both must stay within SLO for a good user experience.
